aliases: Less sugar milk tea with pearls

english: Less sugar milk tea with pearls

ontology.foodex2_description: Tea-based beverages with milk and sweetener
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for bubble tea with tapioca pearls; A0FKP (tea-based beverages with milk) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bubble tea; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is one medium takeaway cup of approximately 500 ml, equivalent to roughly 500 g, as commonly sold at Singaporean bubble tea stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Bubble Tea with Milk (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Bubble Tea with Milk (Less Sugar), cr_id D08010008, confidence 0.95. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are preserved verbatim from the HPB source.

gi_source.title: Tapioca (boiled) and sugar-sweetened milk tea — GI analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for bubble tea with milk (less sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is estimated from the composite of boiled tapioca pearls (GI ~70–85, University of Sydney GI Database) and sweetened milk tea (GI ~55–65). A blended GI of 70 is used as a conservative high-GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 51.45) / 100 ≈ 36.0, based on 500 g serving with 10.29 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar in a sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; sugar content 5.06 g/100 g exceeds the SSB threshold for hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage entirely; the added sugar syrup raises cardiovascular risk independently of the low sodium content (12 mg/100 g), and the 'less sugar' designation does not eliminate the SSB classification (AHA 2018).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with high-GI tapioca pearls (GI ~70); liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose spike regardless of reduced-sugar labelling.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this drink; it is a sugar-sweetened beverage with high-GI tapioca starch, and even the 'less sugar' variant contains added sugar syrup that produces rapid blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Non-dairy creamer likely contains saturated or trans fats; combined with added sugar and high glycaemic load, this beverage contributes to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage; the probable trans and saturated fat content from non-dairy creamer, combined with a high glycaemic load of approximately 36 per serving, makes this a high-risk drink for CVD management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Likely trans fat from non-dairy creamer is strongly pro-inflammatory; added sugar and refined tapioca starch further promote systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this drink; non-dairy creamers with partially hydrogenated oils are strongly pro-inflammatory, and the absence of any anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols) provides no offsetting benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bubble Tea with Milk (Less Sugar) is a freshly prepared Taiwanese-origin beverage widely sold across Singapore at dedicated bubble tea chains and hawker stalls. It consists of brewed black tea blended with non-dairy creamer or milk, a reduced quantity of sugar syrup, and chewy tapioca pearls (boba) at the base. The 'less sugar' designation typically means the sugar syrup is reduced to approximately 25–50% of the standard amount, but added sugar is still present. The drink has a milky, lightly sweet, and mildly astringent tea flavour with a creamy mouthfeel. The tapioca pearls add a distinctive chewy, gummy texture. Per 100 g, it provides 59 kcal, 0.32 g protein, 1.84 g fat, 10.29 g carbohydrate, 5.06 g sugar, 1.16 g dietary fibre, and 12 mg sodium. Despite the reduced sugar label, this remains a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is classified as high-GI (estimated GI ~70) due to the tapioca pearls and residual sugar. It is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Non-dairy creamers used in most commercial preparations may contain saturated or trans fats, adding further cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. It is popular as a casual snack or afternoon drink in Singapore's hot climate.
common_user_queries: Is bubble tea with less sugar healthy?
Can diabetics drink less sugar bubble tea?
How many calories in a cup of milk bubble tea?
Is less sugar boba tea okay for hypertension?
What is the GI of bubble tea?
Is pearl milk tea suitable for weight loss?
Does less sugar bubble tea still have added sugar?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database (cr_id D08010008, confidence 0.95) with exact match. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database using boiled tapioca and sweetened milk tea as reference foods; this is the best available approved-source estimate and is clearly documented.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this freshly prepared commercial bubble tea variant are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry D08010008 or in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite product as of 2026. The input record's dietary_tags of 'low_sodium' (12 mg/100 g) and 'low_calorie' (59 kcal/100 g) are technically accurate per 100 g but are misleading in the context of a 500 ml serving (60 mg sodium, 295 kcal), and these tags have been omitted from the output classification. The occasion_tag 'breakfast' from the input has been replaced with more contextually appropriate meal contexts. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this specific product is not available in any approved source.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built full RESULT JSON from input. Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Expanded aliases with full aliases_by_language. Added health_context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease with clinical guidance. Computed glycemic_load_per_serving as 36.0 based on 500 g serving. Documented all -1 fields in notes. Replaced unsupported GI source with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate. Added ontology with FoodOn exact match and FoodEx2 analogous estimate. Wrote LLM description exceeding 200 characters.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to satisfy G5 hard gate.

Iteration 3: Fixed q8 failure — corrected Hindi alias from 'दू दूध बबल टी (कम चीनी)' to 'दूध बबल टी (कम चीनी)' and performed exhaustive verbatim cross-check of every aliases_by_language string against the flat aliases array. All entries now confirmed consistent. Score 8/8. Decision: PASS.
